⭕ Ringworm in Children

A fungal skin infection, not a worm.
Dr. Murali Gopal
Senior Paediatrician & Paediatric Pulmonologist
MCR: 57489
MBBS, DCH(UK), MRCPCH(UK), FRCPCH(UK), CCT Paediatrics (UK), Fellow in Paediatric Pulmonology (Aus), Allergology (Ind)
Last reviewed: 10 May 2026
Reassurance for parentsRingworm is a common fungal skin infection. It is treatable, but it can spread to other people and sometimes from pets.

What is Ringworm?

Ringworm is a fungal infection that often causes a round or ring-shaped rash with a scaly border. Despite the name, it is not caused by a worm.

Symptoms & Signs

  • Round or ring-shaped rash
  • Scaly or raised edge
  • Itchy skin
  • May affect the body, scalp, feet, or nails
  • Scalp ringworm may cause hair loss or broken hairs

Causes

  • Caused by dermatophyte fungi
  • Spreads by skin contact, shared combs or towels, and sometimes from infected animals
  • Warm, moist skin encourages fungal growth

Home Management

  • Use antifungal treatment exactly as advised.
  • Keep the skin clean and dry.
  • Do not share combs, hats, towels, or clothing.
  • Check family pets if there is concern they may be the source.
  • Scalp ringworm needs medical treatment and should not be treated with routine skin cream alone.

Red Flags / When to Seek Help

  • Scalp involvement
  • Rash spreading quickly or looking infected
  • Pain, pus, or marked swelling
  • No improvement with treatment

Important Facts / Myth correction

Ringworm is a fungus, not a worm.
Skin ringworm often improves with antifungal cream, but scalp ringworm usually needs oral treatment from a doctor.
Good hygiene helps prevent recurrence and spread.

Legal medical disclaimer

This parent guide is for education only and does not replace a medical consultation, diagnosis, or treatment plan. Advice should be interpreted in the context of your child's age, symptoms, and medical history. If your child has worsening symptoms, breathing difficulty, dehydration, unusual drowsiness, persistent high fever, seizures, or any emergency warning signs, seek urgent medical care immediately. All final guides should be reviewed by a qualified medical professional before clinical use.

References

  • Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne. Kids Health Info: Ringworm. https://www.rch.org.au/kidsinfo/fact_sheets/ringworm/
